effects.title: Motion Duration Scale
effects.summary: A small set of duration tokens (instant→slow) mapped to UI scenarios, demonstrated live.
effects.description: Defines --dur-fast/base/slow tokens and shows the same animation at each speed, with guidance on which to use where — the timing half of a motion system.
effects.performance_notes: Trivial.
effects.accessibility_notes: Keep UI transitions short (<300ms) to feel responsive; long durations frustrate. All motion respects prefers-reduced-motion.
variations: Pair each duration with a default easing
effects.ai_usage: The timing half of a motion system. Store --dur-fast (~120ms, hovers/toggles/micro-feedback), --dur-base (~240ms, most transitions, dropdowns), --dur-slow (~400ms, larger surfaces/modals/page-level). Larger/farther-moving elements get longer durations; small UI feedback stays fast. Reference tokens in all transitions for consistency, and combine with the easing tokens. Keep everything under ~400ms for UI.
effects.source:
--dur-fast 120ms
--dur-base 240ms
--dur-slow 400ms

fast = hovers/toggles · base = dropdowns/most transitions · slow = modals/page-level

effects.title: Stagger Choreography
effects.summary: A list whose items animate in one after another with an incremental delay.
effects.description: Demonstrates staggering: the same entrance animation applied to siblings with animation-delay derived from each item's index, so they cascade rather than appear at once — a core motion-design principle.
effects.performance_notes: Transform/opacity per item; cheap. The delay is set via an --i custom property * a step.
effects.accessibility_notes: Keep the total stagger short so content isn't withheld too long. Disable (show all at once) under prefers-reduced-motion.

customization.name: --i
customization.description: Item index (set inline or via nth-child)

variations: Stagger by distance from a focal point
Combine with scroll-reveal so it triggers on entry
effects.ai_usage: Use whenever multiple siblings enter together (menus, lists, grids) — staggering reads as intentional and guides the eye. Apply one entrance animation to all items and set animation-delay: calc(var(--i) * var(--step)) where --i is the item index (inline style or :nth-child). Keep --step small (50-100ms) and the total under ~600ms. Combine with IntersectionObserver to trigger on scroll. Disable under reduced motion. The demo replays on a loop.

effects.title: Animated Dropdown Menu
effects.summary: A dropdown that fades and slides down from its trigger, toggled accessibly on click.
effects.description: A button toggles a menu that animates from a slightly-raised, transparent state into place. Click-outside and Escape close it; aria-expanded is kept in sync.
effects.performance_notes: Transform/opacity transition on a small element; trivial.
effects.accessibility_notes: Trigger is a button with aria-haspopup + aria-expanded; menu items are focusable; Escape closes and returns focus to the trigger; clicking outside closes. For full menu semantics add role=menu/menuitem and arrow-key navigation.
effects.ai_usage: Use for account/overflow menus and selects. Toggle a hidden menu from a button; sync aria-expanded and animate opacity + translateY for the open transition (use visibility/display to keep it out of the tab order when closed). MUST handle: click-outside to close, Escape to close + refocus the trigger, and keyboard access to items. Add role=menu/menuitem and arrow navigation for a true menu widget.

effects.title: Animated Underline Links
effects.summary: Nav links whose underline grows from the center (or slides in) on hover/focus.
effects.description: Uses a scaleX-animated pseudo-element underline with transform-origin so it grows from center, plus a left-to-right slide variant — the cleanest way to animate underlines.
effects.performance_notes: Transform-only on a pseudo-element — GPU-friendly.
effects.accessibility_notes: Apply the same effect on :focus-visible, not just :hover, so keyboard users see it. Keep an accessible color/contrast for the link text itself.
effects.ai_usage: Standard nav/link hover. Link is position:relative; a ::after is an absolutely-positioned 2px bar at the bottom, scaleX(0) with transform-origin:center, transitioning to scaleX(1) on hover AND focus-visible. Change transform-origin to left for a directional slide. Always include :focus-visible for keyboard accessibility.
effects.source: Home Work About Contact

effects.title: Hamburger → X Morph
effects.summary: A three-line menu icon that smoothly morphs into a close (X) when toggled.
effects.description: Three bars transform on toggle: the middle fades, the top and bottom rotate ±45° and translate to form an X — the canonical menu-button animation, on a real accessible button.
effects.performance_notes: Transform/opacity transitions on three spans; trivial.
effects.accessibility_notes: Built on a <button> with aria-expanded and aria-label that updates on toggle — essential for screen readers. Transition removed under prefers-reduced-motion.
variations: Morph to back-arrow instead of X
Spin the whole icon while morphing
effects.ai_usage: Mobile nav toggle. MUST be a real <button> with aria-label and aria-expanded toggled in JS (also drive opening the actual menu from the same state). Three .bar spans; on the .open state, translate+rotate the top/bottom into an X and fade the middle. The top/bottom translate distance equals the gap between bars. Remove transition under reduced motion.

effects.title: Sliding Tab Indicator
effects.summary: A pill/underline indicator that glides to the active tab.
effects.description: A single absolutely-positioned indicator is moved (translate + width) to match the selected tab, giving the smooth 'shared element' tab transition instead of a hard jump.
effects.performance_notes: Animates transform/width of one element; cheap.
effects.accessibility_notes: Use role=tablist/tab/tabpanel and manage aria-selected + roving tabindex; the moving pill is decorative (visual only). Keyboard arrow navigation should move focus between tabs.
variations: Auto-detect active on load from aria-selected
effects.ai_usage: Use for segmented controls and tab bars. Render real tab buttons in a tablist; keep ONE indicator element and on tab change set its transform: translateX(offsetLeft) and width to the active tab's metrics (read from getBoundingClientRect/offsetLeft). Drive selection with proper ARIA (role=tab, aria-selected, arrow-key roving focus); the pill is purely visual. Recompute on resize.

effects.title: Blink Text
effects.summary: The infamous <blink> effect, recreated accessibly with a gentle opacity animation.
effects.description: Reproduces blinking text with a CSS keyframe, but uses a slower fade and a hard stop under reduced-motion instead of the harsh original — a tasteful homage to the most reviled HTML tag.
effects.browser_support: All browsers (CSS version). The <blink> tag is long removed from browsers.
effects.performance_notes: Single opacity animation; trivial.
effects.accessibility_notes: Rapid flashing is a serious WCAG/seizure risk. Keep any blink SLOW (>1s), never use on essential content, and disable entirely under prefers-reduced-motion (handled here). Honestly: prefer not to use blinking at all outside of nostalgia pieces.
variations: Soft fade rather than hard on/off
effects.ai_usage: Nostalgia/retro pieces only. Implement as a slow opacity keyframe (step-end for hard blink, ease for soft), period >= 1s, and ALWAYS disable under prefers-reduced-motion. Never apply to important or interactive content — flashing is an accessibility hazard. Treat this as a deliberate retro flourish, not a UI pattern.
effects.source: UNDER CONSTRUCTION

effects.title: CSS Marquee
effects.summary: A modern, accessible replacement for the deprecated <marquee> tag using CSS animation.
effects.description: Recreates the scrolling-ticker look of the old <marquee> element with a translateX keyframe, pausing on hover and under reduced-motion — without the deprecated tag.
effects.browser_support: All browsers (the CSS version). The original <marquee> tag is deprecated — do not use it.
effects.performance_notes: Single translateX loop; cheap. Duplicate the content for a seamless infinite ticker.
effects.accessibility_notes: Auto-scrolling text is a WCAG concern: provide a pause control (this pauses on hover/focus) and STOP entirely under prefers-reduced-motion. Keep the full text in the DOM for screen readers.
effects.ai_usage: Use for news tickers, stock tapes, and retro homages — never the deprecated <marquee> tag. Wrap content in an overflow:hidden track; the inner element animates translateX(0 → -100%). Duplicate the content inline for a seamless loop. PROVIDE A PAUSE (hover/focus + a real button in production) and disable under prefers-reduced-motion for accessibility.
effects.source: ★ Welcome to my homepage ★ Best viewed in 800x600 ★ Sign my guestbook! ★

effects.title: Rainbow Cycling Text
effects.summary: Text that cycles through the rainbow via an animated hue rotation — peak GeoCities energy.
effects.description: Fills text with a rainbow gradient (background-clip) and animates the hue with a filter or moving gradient, recreating the animated rainbow text of the early personal web.
effects.performance_notes: Animating hue-rotate filter or background-position; cheap for a heading.
effects.accessibility_notes: Rainbow text can be low-contrast and hard to read; keep it to large display headings, not body copy. Animation stops under prefers-reduced-motion (static rainbow remains).
variations: Pair with a comic-sans retro vibe
effects.ai_usage: Retro/playful headings and easter eggs. Apply a rainbow linear-gradient clipped to text, then animate filter: hue-rotate(0 → 360deg) for the cycling. Keep to large text for readability and disable animation under reduced motion. For a per-letter wave, wrap letters in spans and stagger animation-delay.
effects.source: ~ Welcome ~

effects.title: Starfield Warp
effects.summary: Stars streaking toward the viewer — the screensaver/space-warp staple, on canvas.
effects.description: A canvas animation projects randomized 3D star points toward the camera and resets them when they pass, producing the classic flying-through-space starfield.
effects.performance_notes: Canvas with a few hundred points at 60fps is light. Cap star count and use a single clear per frame. Stop the rAF loop when offscreen in production.
effects.accessibility_notes: Decorative full-screen motion; halt the loop and show a static frame under prefers-reduced-motion. Keep foreground text on a contrasted surface.
variations: Slower drift for an ambient sky
effects.ai_usage: Hero/space-themed backgrounds and intros. Canvas sized to the container; each star has x,y in [-1,1] and z depth; per frame decrease z (move toward camera), project to screen as x/z, y/z, draw a dot whose size grows as z shrinks; respawn at far z when it passes. Use canvas (not DOM) for hundreds of points. Stop the loop / show static under reduced motion and when offscreen.
effects.source:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8" />
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" />
  <title>Starfield Warp</title>
  <script type="application/json" id="effect-meta">
  {
    "id": "starfield",
    "title": "Starfield Warp",
    "summary": "Stars streaking toward the viewer — the screensaver/space-warp staple, on canvas.",
    "description": "A canvas animation projects randomized 3D star points toward the camera and resets them when they pass, producing the classic flying-through-space starfield.",
    "theme": "retro-web",
    "categories": ["retro", "canvas", "background"],
    "tags": ["starfield", "warp", "canvas", "space", "screensaver", "particles"],
    "tech": ["canvas", "js"],
    "era": "2000s",
    "difficulty": "advanced",
    "dependencies": [],
    "browser_support": "All modern browsers (canvas 2D).",
    "performance_notes": "Canvas with a few hundred points at 60fps is light. Cap star count and use a single clear per frame. Stop the rAF loop when offscreen in production.",
    "accessibility_notes": "Decorative full-screen motion; halt the loop and show a static frame under prefers-reduced-motion. Keep foreground text on a contrasted surface.",
    "customization": [
      { "name": "STAR_COUNT", "description": "Number of stars", "default": "400" },
      { "name": "SPEED", "description": "Warp speed", "default": "0.02" }
    ],
    "variations": ["Colored/hyperspace streaks (draw trails)", "Slower drift for an ambient sky", "Mouse-steerable camera"],
    "related": ["floating-particles"],
    "ai_usage": "Hero/space-themed backgrounds and intros. Canvas sized to the container; each star has x,y in [-1,1] and z depth; per frame decrease z (move toward camera), project to screen as x/z, y/z, draw a dot whose size grows as z shrinks; respawn at far z when it passes. Use canvas (not DOM) for hundreds of points. Stop the loop / show static under reduced motion and when offscreen."
  }
  </script>
  <style>
    html, body { height: 100%; margin: 0; overflow: hidden; background: #000; }
    canvas { display: block; }
  </style>
</head>
<body>
  <canvas id="c"></canvas>
  <script>
    var c = document.getElementById("c"), ctx = c.getContext("2d");
    var STAR_COUNT = 400, SPEED = 0.02, stars = [];
    var reduce = matchMedia("(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)").matches;
    function resize() { c.width = innerWidth; c.height = innerHeight; }
    resize(); addEventListener("resize", resize);
    function spawn() { return { x: Math.random() * 2 - 1, y: Math.random() * 2 - 1, z: Math.random() }; }
    for (var i = 0; i < STAR_COUNT; i++) stars.push(spawn());
    function draw() {
      ctx.fillStyle = "#000"; ctx.fillRect(0, 0, c.width, c.height);
      var cx = c.width / 2, cy = c.height / 2;
      for (var i = 0; i < stars.length; i++) {
        var s = stars[i];
        if (!reduce) { s.z -= SPEED; if (s.z <= 0.01) stars[i] = spawn(); }
        var sx = cx + (s.x / s.z) * cx, sy = cy + (s.y / s.z) * cy;
        var r = (1 - s.z) * 2.5;
        ctx.fillStyle = "rgba(255,255,255," + (1 - s.z) + ")";
        ctx.beginPath(); ctx.arc(sx, sy, r, 0, Math.PI * 2); ctx.fill();
      }
      if (!reduce) requestAnimationFrame(draw);
    }
    draw();
  </script>
</body>
</html>

effects.title: Image Clip Reveal on Scroll
effects.summary: An image unveils behind a clip-path wipe as it scrolls into view.
effects.description: Combines IntersectionObserver with a clip-path inset transition so the image is wiped into view (rather than just faded) when it enters the viewport — a premium editorial reveal.
effects.performance_notes: Animating clip-path is GPU-friendly; the observer fires once per element.
effects.accessibility_notes: Image is in the DOM regardless; under prefers-reduced-motion show it immediately (no clip). Provide real alt text.
variations: Combine with a slight scale for depth
effects.ai_usage: Use for figure/image reveals in articles and case studies. Start the image clipped (clip-path: inset(0 100% 0 0)) and transition to inset(0) when an IntersectionObserver adds the .in class. Change which inset side is 100% to set wipe direction. Short-circuit to fully revealed under prefers-reduced-motion. The demo auto-scrolls so the wipe is visible in the catalog frame.

effects.title: Parallax Layers
effects.summary: Background layers scroll slower than foreground content for a depth illusion — pure CSS.
effects.description: Uses CSS 3D transforms (perspective + translateZ + scale) on a scroll container so deeper layers move slower, achieving parallax without JS scroll listeners.
effects.browser_support: All modern browsers (the perspective-scroll technique). transform-style support required.
effects.performance_notes: Compositor-driven; far smoother than JS scroll parallax. Watch out for interfering with position:fixed children.
effects.accessibility_notes: Parallax can cause motion discomfort — collapse to a flat scroll under prefers-reduced-motion.

customization.name: translateZ depth
customization.description: Negative Z + scale per layer controls parallax strength

variations: JS variant with rAF for fine control
effects.ai_usage: Hero/storytelling sections with depth. The container gets perspective + overflow scroll; layers are positioned and pushed back with translateZ(negative) then scaled up to refill the viewport — deeper (more negative Z) = slower scroll. Keep the readable content at Z:0. Always provide a reduced-motion flat fallback (remove transforms). The demo auto-scrolls so parallax shows in the frame.
effects.source:
Parallax
Keep scrolling…

effects.title: Scroll Progress Bar
effects.summary: A top bar that fills as the reader scrolls, using pure-CSS scroll-driven animation.
effects.description: Uses the modern animation-timeline: scroll() to scale a fixed bar from 0 to 1 across the page scroll — no JS. Includes a JS fallback note for older browsers.
effects.browser_support: Chromium 115+ and modern browsers support scroll-driven animations; older browsers ignore the timeline (bar stays empty) — provide a tiny JS fallback in production.
effects.performance_notes: Scroll-driven animations run off the main thread — smoother and cheaper than scroll-event JS.
effects.accessibility_notes: Decorative reading indicator; safe under reduced-motion (it tracks position, not autonomous motion). Don't convey essential info with it alone.
effects.ai_usage: Reading progress for articles/long pages. Preferred: a fixed bar with transform-origin:left, animation: grow linear, animation-timeline: scroll(root block) — pure CSS, no JS. For browser support, add a JS fallback that sets scaleX = scrollTop / (scrollHeight - innerHeight) on scroll. The demo scrolls its own inner container so it animates in the catalog frame.

effects.title: Scroll Snap Gallery
effects.summary: A horizontal gallery where each slide snaps cleanly into place — pure CSS scroll-snap.
effects.description: Uses scroll-snap-type on a horizontally-scrolling container and scroll-snap-align on children so swiping/scrolling locks each slide to the start, a native carousel with no JS.
effects.performance_notes: Native scrolling; very smooth and JS-free.
effects.accessibility_notes: Keep slides keyboard-scrollable and focusable; native scroll handles touch/trackpad. For a true carousel add labels and optional prev/next buttons. Snap doesn't trap focus.
effects.ai_usage: Use for image carousels, onboarding slides, and full-page section snapping. Container: overflow-x:auto; scroll-snap-type: x mandatory; flex row. Each slide: flex:0 0 <width>; scroll-snap-align: start (or center). Use 'proximity' instead of 'mandatory' if mandatory feels too aggressive. Add prev/next buttons calling scrollBy for a fuller carousel; native scroll already gives touch/keyboard support.

effects.title: Sticky Stacking Cards
effects.summary: Cards stick to the top and stack on one another as you scroll past them.
effects.description: Each card uses position: sticky with the same top offset so successive cards pin and overlap, building a layered stack — a trendy storytelling/section pattern requiring no JS.
effects.performance_notes: Pure layout/paint; very efficient, no scroll JS.
effects.accessibility_notes: Content remains in natural reading order; safe under reduced-motion (no autonomous animation).
variations: Scale/rotate each card slightly as it stacks (scroll-driven)
Offset top per card for a fanned stack
effects.ai_usage: Use for feature walkthroughs and editorial sections. Give each card position:sticky; top:<offset>; the cards naturally pin and overlap as you scroll. Add a slight scale-down via scroll-driven animation for the 'deck' feel. Ensure enough scroll distance between cards. The demo auto-scrolls to show the stacking in the catalog frame.

effects.title: SVG Animated Gradient Stroke
effects.summary: A circular progress/loader ring stroked with a gradient and an animating dash.
effects.description: Combines an SVG linearGradient stroke with stroke-linecap round and an animated stroke-dasharray/dashoffset to create a smooth gradient-stroked spinner or progress arc.
effects.browser_support: All modern browsers. Note: SVG gradient stroke doesn't rotate with the element, so rotate the whole SVG, not the gradient.
effects.performance_notes: Animating rotation + dash via CSS is cheap.
effects.accessibility_notes: If used as a loader, wrap with role=status + a visually-hidden label. Slow under prefers-reduced-motion rather than stopping (it's a busy indicator).
variations: Determinate progress (set dashoffset from a value)
effects.ai_usage: Use for premium loaders and progress rings. Stroke a <circle> with url(#gradient); set stroke-linecap='round'. For an indeterminate spinner, animate the whole SVG's rotation AND the stroke-dasharray/dashoffset (Material-style). For determinate progress, fix dasharray to the circumference and set dashoffset from your percentage. Rotate the SVG element, not the gradient (gradients are in user space). Add role=status + label when it's a loader.

effects.title: SVG Gooey Filter
effects.summary: Blobs that merge with a liquid 'metaball' connection using an SVG blur + contrast filter.
effects.description: The classic gooey effect: feGaussianBlur softens shapes, then feColorMatrix cranks alpha contrast so overlapping blurred edges snap together into a liquid blob. Applied to CSS-animated circles.
effects.browser_support: All modern browsers. The filter can be defined in a 0-size inline SVG and referenced from CSS via filter: url(#goo).
effects.performance_notes: SVG blur filters are moderately expensive; keep the filtered area small and the element count low. Motion is CSS transform (cheap).
effects.accessibility_notes: Purely decorative; blob motion stops under prefers-reduced-motion. The contrast matrix can produce hard edges — keep it off text.

customization.name: feColorMatrix alpha row
customization.description: The '18 -7' values tune the snap threshold

variations: Gooey menu (FAB that spits out items)
Colored goo with a gradient fill
effects.ai_usage: Use for playful liquid UI: gooey menus, merging loader dots, blob backgrounds. Define filter#goo = feGaussianBlur(stdDeviation ~10) → feColorMatrix(... 18 -7) → feComposite. Apply filter:url(#goo) to a CONTAINER whose children (solid circles) overlap; the blur+contrast makes them fuse. Tune stdDeviation for goo length and the matrix's last two alpha numbers for the merge threshold. Keep it off text and gate motion under reduced-motion.

effects.title: SVG Grain / Noise Texture
effects.summary: A subtle film-grain overlay generated entirely by feTurbulence — no image asset.
effects.description: feTurbulence produces fractal noise rendered to a rect, used as a low-opacity overlay to add tactile grain over flat colors and gradients (the trendy 'noise' texture), with zero network cost.
effects.performance_notes: Generating noise once is cheap; do NOT animate it (re-rendering noise every frame is expensive). Reuse one noise layer as a fixed overlay.
effects.accessibility_notes: Purely decorative; keep opacity low so it never reduces text contrast. No motion.
variations: Heavier grain for a poster look
Scope the overlay to one card
effects.ai_usage: Use to add tactile grain over flat fills, gradients, and mesh backgrounds (kills banding, adds a premium analog feel). Generate noise with feTurbulence(type='fractalNoise', baseFrequency ~0.8, numOctaves~3); render it to a full-size rect; place that as a position:fixed/absolute overlay at low opacity (0.04-0.08), pointer-events:none, above the background but below text. Do NOT animate the turbulence (costly) — a static layer is enough. Optionally desaturate it with feColorMatrix.
effects.source: Grain over gradient

effects.title: SVG Line Draw
effects.summary: A path that draws itself on, like a pen tracing a signature or logo.
effects.description: Sets stroke-dasharray and stroke-dashoffset to the path length, then animates the offset to 0 with CSS so the stroke appears to be drawn. The canonical SVG entrance for logos, signatures, and line icons.
effects.performance_notes: Animating stroke-dashoffset is GPU-friendly. Set the dasharray/offset to the path's total length (pathLength attribute lets you use a round number like 100).
effects.accessibility_notes: Decorative; give the SVG role=img + a title/aria-label if it conveys meaning. Animation is disabled under prefers-reduced-motion (path shown complete).

customization.name: pathLength
customization.description: Normalizes length so dasharray can be 100

variations: Stagger multiple paths to draw in sequence
Fill the shape in after the stroke completes
effects.ai_usage: Use for logo/signature reveals and line-icon entrances. Add pathLength='100' to each path so you can set stroke-dasharray:100 and animate stroke-dashoffset from 100 to 0 in CSS. Multiple paths: give each an animation-delay to draw in order. To 'ink in' a filled logo, fade fill from transparent to solid after the stroke finishes. Gate with prefers-reduced-motion (show offset:0).

  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" />
  <title>SVG Line Draw</title>
  <script type="application/json" id="effect-meta">
  {
    "id": "svg-line-draw",
    "title": "SVG Line Draw",
    "summary": "A path that draws itself on, like a pen tracing a signature or logo.",
    "description": "Sets stroke-dasharray and stroke-dashoffset to the path length, then animates the offset to 0 with CSS so the stroke appears to be drawn. The canonical SVG entrance for logos, signatures, and line icons.",
    "theme": "svg-effects",
    "categories": ["svg", "animation", "stroke"],
    "tags": ["svg", "line-draw", "stroke-dashoffset", "signature", "logo", "draw-on"],
    "tech": ["svg", "css"],
    "era": "2010s",
    "difficulty": "intermediate",
    "dependencies": [],
    "browser_support": "All modern browsers.",
    "performance_notes": "Animating stroke-dashoffset is GPU-friendly. Set the dasharray/offset to the path's total length (pathLength attribute lets you use a round number like 100).",
    "accessibility_notes": "Decorative; give the SVG role=img + a title/aria-label if it conveys meaning. Animation is disabled under prefers-reduced-motion (path shown complete).",
    "customization": [
      { "name": "pathLength", "description": "Normalizes length so dasharray can be 100", "default": "100" },
      { "name": "--dur", "description": "Draw duration", "default": "2.5s" }
    ],
    "variations": ["Stagger multiple paths to draw in sequence", "Fill the shape in after the stroke completes", "Loop by alternating the offset"],
    "related": ["svg-animated-checkmark", "typewriter-text"],
    "ai_usage": "Use for logo/signature reveals and line-icon entrances. Add pathLength='100' to each path so you can set stroke-dasharray:100 and animate stroke-dashoffset from 100 to 0 in CSS. Multiple paths: give each an animation-delay to draw in order. To 'ink in' a filled logo, fade fill from transparent to solid after the stroke finishes. Gate with prefers-reduced-motion (show offset:0)."
  }
  </script>
  <style>
    :root { --dur: 2.5s; }
    html, body { height: 100%; margin: 0; }
    body { display: grid; place-items: center; background: #0b0d12; }
    .draw path { stroke: #7c5cff; stroke-width: 3; fill: none; stroke-linecap: round; stroke-linejoin: round;
      stroke-dasharray: 100; stroke-dashoffset: 100; animation: draw var(--dur) ease forwards infinite; }
    .draw path:nth-child(2) { stroke: #2dd4bf; animation-delay: .4s; }
    .draw path:nth-child(3) { stroke: #f0abfc; animation-delay: .8s; }
    @keyframes draw { 0% { stroke-dashoffset: 100; } 70%, 100% { stroke-dashoffset: 0; } }
    @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) { .draw path { animation: none; stroke-dashoffset: 0; } }
  </style>
</head>
<body>
  <svg class="draw" width="220" height="160" viewBox="0 0 220 160" role="img" aria-label="Drawing line art">
    <path pathLength="100" d="M20,120 C40,40 80,40 100,120 S160,40 200,120" />
    <path pathLength="100" d="M20,80 H200" />
    <path pathLength="100" d="M110,20 V140" />
  </svg>
</body>
</html>

effects.title: SVG Path Morph
effects.summary: One shape smoothly morphs into another by animating the path's d attribute (SMIL).
effects.description: Uses SMIL <animate attributeName='d'> to tween a path between several shapes. Morphing requires each shape's path to have the SAME command structure / point count.
effects.browser_support: SMIL animate of `d` works in Chrome, Firefox, and Safari. (CSS `d` interpolation is Chromium-only; SMIL is the most portable no-JS option.)
effects.performance_notes: Cheap for a single path. The hard part is authoring compatible paths, not runtime cost.
effects.accessibility_notes: SMIL does NOT honor prefers-reduced-motion automatically — the included script pauses SVG animations when reduced motion is requested. Decorative; add a title if meaningful.

customization.name: values
customization.description: Semicolon-separated d strings to morph between

variations: Morph icons (play↔pause) on state change via JS
Use a JS lib (flubber) when point counts can't match
effects.ai_usage: Use to tween between shapes/icons. CRITICAL: every shape in the `values` list must share the same path command sequence and point count, or the morph jumps. Author them by editing one base path's coordinates. SMIL <animate attributeName='d' values='A;B;C;A' dur=... repeatCount='indefinite'> is the most cross-browser no-JS route. For arbitrary shapes with mismatched points, use a JS morphing lib (flubber/GSAP). SMIL ignores reduced-motion, so pause via svg.pauseAnimations() (shown).

    "performance_notes": "Cheap for a single path. The hard part is authoring compatible paths, not runtime cost.",
    "accessibility_notes": "SMIL does NOT honor prefers-reduced-motion automatically — the included script pauses SVG animations when reduced motion is requested. Decorative; add a title if meaningful.",
    "customization": [
      { "name": "values", "description": "Semicolon-separated d strings to morph between", "default": "see source" },

Author them by editing one base path's coordinates. SMIL <animate attributeName='d' values='A;B;C;A' dur=... repeatCount='indefinite'> is the most cross-browser no-JS route. For arbitrary shapes with mismatched points, use a JS morphing lib (flubber/GSAP). SMIL ignores reduced-motion, so pause via svg.pauseAnimations() (shown)."
  }
  </script>
  <style>

  <svg width="220" height="220" viewBox="0 0 200 200" role="img" aria-label="Morphing shape">

  </svg>

    if (matchMedia("(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)").matches) {

effects.title: SVG Success Checkmark
effects.summary: A success animation: the circle draws around, then the tick strokes in, with a pop.
effects.description: Two stroked SVG paths (circle + check) animate their stroke-dashoffset in sequence, plus a subtle scale pop — the satisfying confirmation seen after form submits and payments.
effects.performance_notes: Two stroke-dashoffset animations + one transform; trivial.
effects.accessibility_notes: Pair with a text message and aria-live so success isn't conveyed by the icon alone. Under prefers-reduced-motion, show the completed check instantly.
variations: Replace a loading spinner on completion
effects.ai_usage: Use to confirm completed async actions (submit, pay, save), ideally swapping in where a spinner was. Stroke a circle path and a check path; animate each one's stroke-dashoffset from full to 0, the tick delayed until the circle finishes; add a small scale pop. ALWAYS accompany with a text status + aria-live region — color/icon alone isn't accessible. Show the final state instantly under reduced motion. Demo loops for visibility.
effects.source:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8" />
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" />
  <title>SVG Success Checkmark</title>
  <script type="application/json" id="effect-meta">
  {
    "id": "svg-animated-checkmark",
    "title": "SVG Success Checkmark",
    "summary": "A success animation: the circle draws around, then the tick strokes in, with a pop.",
    "description": "Two stroked SVG paths (circle + check) animate their stroke-dashoffset in sequence, plus a subtle scale pop — the satisfying confirmation seen after form submits and payments.",
    "theme": "svg-effects",
    "categories": ["svg", "animation", "feedback"],
    "tags": ["svg", "checkmark", "success", "confirmation", "stroke", "tick"],
    "tech": ["svg", "css"],
    "era": "2020s",
    "difficulty": "intermediate",
    "dependencies": [],
    "browser_support": "All modern browsers.",
    "performance_notes": "Two stroke-dashoffset animations + one transform; trivial.",
    "accessibility_notes": "Pair with a text message and aria-live so success isn't conveyed by the icon alone. Under prefers-reduced-motion, show the completed check instantly.",
    "customization": [
      { "name": "--ok", "description": "Success color", "default": "#22c55e" },
      { "name": "timing", "description": "Circle then tick durations/delays", "default": "0.5s + 0.3s" }
    ],
    "variations": ["Error variant (red circle + X)", "Replace a loading spinner on completion", "Bounce the whole badge in"],
    "related": ["svg-line-draw", "animated-checkbox", "loading-button"],
    "ai_usage": "Use to confirm completed async actions (submit, pay, save), ideally swapping in where a spinner was. Stroke a circle path and a check path; animate each one's stroke-dashoffset from full to 0, the tick delayed until the circle finishes; add a small scale pop. ALWAYS accompany with a text status + aria-live region — color/icon alone isn't accessible. Show the final state instantly under reduced motion. Demo loops for visibility."
  }
  </script>
  <style>
    :root { --ok: #22c55e; }
    html, body { height: 100%; margin: 0; }
    body { display: grid; place-items: center; background: #0b0d12; }
    .check { animation: pop .4s ease both; }
    .circle { stroke: var(--ok); stroke-width: 5; fill: none; stroke-dasharray: 166; stroke-dashoffset: 166;
      animation: stroke .5s cubic-bezier(.65,0,.45,1) forwards; }
    .tick { stroke: var(--ok); stroke-width: 6; fill: none; stroke-linecap: round; stroke-linejoin: round;
      stroke-dasharray: 48; stroke-dashoffset: 48; animation: stroke .3s cubic-bezier(.65,0,.45,1) .5s forwards; }
    @keyframes stroke { to { stroke-dashoffset: 0; } }
    @keyframes pop { 0% { transform: scale(.7); } 60% { transform: scale(1.05); } 100% { transform: scale(1); } }
    @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) {
      .circle, .tick { animation: none; stroke-dashoffset: 0; } .check { animation: none; }
    }
  </style>
</head>
<body>
  <svg class="check" width="120" height="120" viewBox="0 0 60 60" role="img" aria-label="Success">
    <circle class="circle" cx="30" cy="30" r="26" />
    <path class="tick" d="M18,31 l8,8 l16,-17" />
  </svg>
  <script>
    // Replay on a loop so the draw-in is visible in the catalog frame.
    if (!matchMedia("(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)").matches) {
      var svg = document.querySelector(".check");
      setInterval(function () {
        svg.querySelectorAll(".circle,.tick").forEach(function (p) { p.style.animation = "none"; });
        svg.style.animation = "none"; void svg.offsetWidth;
        svg.querySelectorAll(".circle,.tick").forEach(function (p) { p.style.animation = ""; });
        svg.style.animation = "";
      }, 2600);
    }
  </script>
</body>
</html>

effects.title: SVG Text on a Path
effects.summary: Text that flows along a curve, optionally scrolling around it — circular badges and arched headings.
effects.description: Uses <textPath href='#path'> to lay text along an arbitrary path, with an animated startOffset to make it travel — perfect for circular seal/badge text and curved labels.
effects.browser_support: All modern browsers (textPath). startOffset animation via SMIL works broadly.
effects.performance_notes: Cheap. Define the path once and reference it; hide the path stroke if you only want the text.
effects.accessibility_notes: SVG text is real, selectable, and readable by screen readers — better than image text. Add role=img + aria-label on the SVG if the curved text is part of a logo. SMIL motion ignores reduced-motion; the script pauses it.

customization.name: path d
customization.description: The curve the text follows (arc, circle, wave)

customization.name: startOffset
customization.description: Where text begins along the path (animate to scroll)

variations: Text along a wavy or spiral path
effects.ai_usage: Use for circular badges/seals, arched titles, and curved labels. Define a <path id='p'> (a circle drawn as a path for a ring of text, or an arc for an arch) and put <text><textPath href='#p'>…</textPath></text>. Set the path's stroke to none if you only want text. To rotate the text around the ring, animate startOffset 0%→100% (SMIL) — pause under reduced motion. SVG text stays accessible/selectable.

    "customization": [
      { "name": "path d", "description": "The curve the text follows (arc, circle, wave)", "default": "circle path" },

To rotate the text around the ring, animate startOffset 0%→100% (SMIL) — pause under reduced motion. SVG text stays accessible/selectable."

  <style>

effects.title: SVG Turbulence Distortion
effects.summary: A wavy, liquid distortion driven by feTurbulence + feDisplacementMap, animated.
effects.description: feTurbulence generates Perlin-style noise that feDisplacementMap uses to push pixels around, creating a rippling/heat-haze/liquid warp. An animated baseFrequency makes the distortion flow.
effects.browser_support: All modern browsers support the filter primitives. Animating baseFrequency via SMIL works broadly.
effects.performance_notes: Displacement filters are GPU-heavy at large sizes — keep the filtered region modest and the scale low. Static distortion is much cheaper than animated.
effects.accessibility_notes: Decorative. SMIL ignores reduced-motion; the script pauses animation when requested. Avoid distorting readable text.
variations: Heat-haze on a hero image (low scale)
Hover-only distortion via JS toggling the filter
effects.ai_usage: Use for liquid/wavy/heat-haze treatments on shapes, images, or logos. Pipeline: feTurbulence(type='fractalNoise', baseFrequency=low) → feDisplacementMap(in='SourceGraphic', scale=N). Increase scale for stronger warp; animate baseFrequency (SMIL) to make it flow. Keep scale modest on text or it becomes unreadable, and keep the area small for performance. Pause via svg.pauseAnimations() under reduced motion (shown).
effects.source:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8" />
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" />
  <title>SVG Turbulence Distortion</title>
  <script type="application/json" id="effect-meta">
  {
    "id": "svg-turbulence-distortion",
    "title": "SVG Turbulence Distortion",
    "summary": "A wavy, liquid distortion driven by feTurbulence + feDisplacementMap, animated.",
    "description": "feTurbulence generates Perlin-style noise that feDisplacementMap uses to push pixels around, creating a rippling/heat-haze/liquid warp. An animated baseFrequency makes the distortion flow.",
    "theme": "svg-effects",
    "categories": ["svg", "filter", "distortion"],
    "tags": ["svg", "feturbulence", "displacement", "distortion", "ripple", "liquid", "noise"],
    "tech": ["svg"],
    "era": "2020s",
    "difficulty": "advanced",
    "dependencies": [],
    "browser_support": "All modern browsers support the filter primitives. Animating baseFrequency via SMIL works broadly.",
    "performance_notes": "Displacement filters are GPU-heavy at large sizes — keep the filtered region modest and the scale low. Static distortion is much cheaper than animated.",
    "accessibility_notes": "Decorative. SMIL ignores reduced-motion; the script pauses animation when requested. Avoid distorting readable text.",
    "customization": [
      { "name": "feDisplacementMap scale", "description": "Distortion strength", "default": "18" },
      { "name": "baseFrequency", "description": "Noise scale (smaller = larger waves)", "default": "0.012" }
    ],
    "variations": ["Heat-haze on a hero image (low scale)", "Flag wave on text/logo", "Hover-only distortion via JS toggling the filter"],
    "related": ["svg-noise-texture", "blob-morph"],
    "ai_usage": "Use for liquid/wavy/heat-haze treatments on shapes, images, or logos. Pipeline: feTurbulence(type='fractalNoise', baseFrequency=low) → feDisplacementMap(in='SourceGraphic', scale=N). Increase scale for stronger warp; animate baseFrequency (SMIL) to make it flow. Keep scale modest on text or it becomes unreadable, and keep the area small for performance. Pause via svg.pauseAnimations() under reduced motion (shown)."
  }
  </script>
  <style>
    html, body { height: 100%; margin: 0; }
    body { display: grid; place-items: center; background: #06060c; font-family: system-ui, sans-serif; }
  </style>
</head>
<body>
  <svg width="300" height="200" viewBox="0 0 300 200" role="img" aria-label="Distorted shape">
    <defs>
      <filter id="warp">
        <feTurbulence type="fractalNoise" baseFrequency="0.012 0.02" numOctaves="2" seed="7" result="noise">
          <animate attributeName="baseFrequency" dur="14s" repeatCount="indefinite"
            values="0.012 0.02; 0.02 0.012; 0.012 0.02" />
        </feTurbulence>
        <feDisplacementMap in="SourceGraphic" in2="noise" scale="18" xChannelSelector="R" yChannelSelector="G" />
      </filter>
      <linearGradient id="wg" x1="0" y1="0" x2="1" y2="1">
        <stop offset="0%" stop-color="#7c5cff" /><stop offset="100%" stop-color="#2dd4bf" />
      </linearGradient>
    </defs>
    <g filter="url(#warp)">
      <rect x="40" y="40" width="220" height="120" rx="20" fill="url(#wg)" />
      <text x="150" y="112" text-anchor="middle" font-size="40" font-weight="800" fill="#fff" font-family="system-ui, sans-serif">LIQUID</text>
    </g>
  </svg>
  <script>
    if (matchMedia("(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)").matches) {
      document.querySelectorAll("svg").forEach(function (s) { if (s.pauseAnimations) s.pauseAnimations(); });
    }
  </script>
</body>
</html>

effects.title: Animated Gradient Text
effects.summary: Letters filled with a moving multi-stop gradient via background-clip: text.
effects.description: Paints a wide linear-gradient as the element background, clips it to the glyph shapes, and animates background-position to make the colors flow through the text.
effects.browser_support: All modern browsers. Needs -webkit-background-clip: text (still required even in Chromium).
effects.performance_notes: Animating background-position is GPU-friendly and cheap.
effects.accessibility_notes: The text remains real, selectable text. Animation pauses under prefers-reduced-motion. Verify the lightest gradient stop has enough contrast.
variations: Conic gradient for a rainbow sweep
Pair with a transparent text-stroke outline
effects.ai_usage: Ideal for headings, brand names, and marketing hero text. Apply .grad-text to an inline-block heading. Set --grad to your palette and keep the gradient at least 2x wider than the text (background-size:200%) so the animation has room to travel. Works on any background. Remove the animation property for a static gradient fill.
effects.source: Gradient

effects.title: Glitch Text
effects.summary: RGB-split datamosh glitch using duplicated pseudo-elements and clip-path jitter.
effects.description: Duplicates the text via ::before and ::after offset in cyan and magenta, then animates clip-path bands to create a chromatic-aberration / signal-glitch effect.
effects.browser_support: All modern browsers (clip-path inset is widely supported).
effects.performance_notes: Two extra painted layers plus clip-path animation; fine for a few headings, avoid on long body text.
effects.accessibility_notes: Can be distracting/seizure-adjacent; the motion stops under prefers-reduced-motion, leaving a static RGB-split. The text is duplicated via data-text and aria stays on the real element.
variations: Increase clip band frequency for heavier corruption
Add skew transforms for VHS feel
effects.ai_usage: Use for cyberpunk/gaming/tech headlines. Requires the text to be duplicated in a data-text attribute that matches the visible text exactly (the pseudo-elements read content: attr(data-text)). Apply .glitch to a heading. Best on dark backgrounds. Consider gating the animation behind :hover for calmer pages.
effects.source: GLITCH

effects.title: Neon Glow Text
effects.summary: Glowing neon-sign lettering built from layered text-shadows with a soft flicker.
effects.description: Stacks several increasingly-blurred text-shadows in the brand color to fake the bloom of a gas neon tube, plus an optional flicker animation.
effects.browser_support: All browsers; text-shadow is universally supported.
effects.performance_notes: Multiple text-shadows are cheap. The flicker animates opacity/text-shadow on a single element — negligible cost.
effects.accessibility_notes: Ensure sufficient contrast against the dark background. The flicker is disabled under prefers-reduced-motion.
variations: Swap --neon per word for multi-color signage
Remove the flicker for a steady tube
effects.ai_usage: Use for hero headings, logos, or playful CTAs with a retro/cyberpunk vibe on dark backgrounds. Apply the .neon class to any inline text element. Tune --neon for the tube color and keep the background dark for the glow to read. The glow is purely text-shadow, so it scales with font-size automatically. Always keep the prefers-reduced-motion guard if you keep the flicker.
effects.source: NEON

effects.title: Outline / Stroke Text
effects.summary: Hollow outlined lettering that fills with color on hover, via text-stroke.
effects.description: Uses -webkit-text-stroke with a transparent fill for hollow display text, then transitions to a solid fill on hover — a bold editorial/poster treatment.
effects.browser_support: All modern browsers via -webkit-text-stroke (works cross-browser despite the prefix).
effects.performance_notes: Static + color transition; trivial.
effects.accessibility_notes: Hollow text can be low-contrast — keep stroke thick enough and the background plain. Provide a solid-fill fallback for very small sizes where strokes get muddy.
effects.ai_usage: Use for large display headings, posters, and section numbers. Set -webkit-text-stroke: WIDTH COLOR and color: transparent for a hollow look; transition color to a solid fill on hover/scroll for emphasis. Keep to big, bold weights — thin/small text loses legibility. Ensure contrast and provide a solid fallback at small sizes.
effects.source: BOLD

effects.title: Shimmer / Shine Text
effects.summary: A bright highlight sweeps across muted text like light catching metal — the classic skeleton/shine loop.
effects.description: Uses a gradient that is mostly the base color with a narrow bright band, clipped to the text, and slides the band across with background-position.
effects.performance_notes: Single element, animates background-position — very cheap.
effects.accessibility_notes: Decorative; animation pauses under prefers-reduced-motion leaving readable text. Keep the base color readable on its own.
variations: Gold/silver palette for a metallic plaque
Slow it down for a luxury feel
effects.ai_usage: Two uses: (1) decorative shiny headings, (2) loading-state text placeholders. Apply .shimmer to text. The trick is a 3-stop gradient (base, shine, base) sized larger than the element so the shine band can travel. For loading skeletons, apply the same technique to a gray block instead of text.
effects.source: Loading…

effects.title: Text Reveal Mask
effects.summary: Lines of text slide up from behind a clipping mask, like film titles.
effects.description: Each line sits in an overflow:hidden wrapper; the inner span is translated from below to its resting position with a staggered delay, producing an elegant editorial reveal.
effects.performance_notes: Animates transform/opacity only — GPU-friendly. Stagger via animation-delay.
effects.accessibility_notes: Text is in the DOM and readable; animation collapses to an instant show under prefers-reduced-motion.
variations: Add a slight rotation for a flip-up
effects.ai_usage: Use for hero headlines and section intros. Structure: each line is …; the outer clips, the inner animates translateY from --rise to 0. Stagger lines by multiplying --stagger by line index (inline style or :nth-child). For scroll-triggered reveals, add the animating class when the element enters the viewport instead of on load.
effects.source: Designed in motion, revealed line by line.

effects.title: Typewriter Text
effects.summary: Text types itself out with a blinking caret using the steps() timing function.
effects.description: Animates max-width / width from 0 with steps() so characters appear one glyph at a time, plus a border-right caret that blinks.
effects.browser_support: All modern browsers. The pure-CSS version needs a known character count and a monospace font.
effects.performance_notes: Animating width triggers layout but on a single short line it is negligible.
effects.accessibility_notes: Full text is present in the DOM immediately (just visually clipped), so screen readers get it. Animation is skipped under prefers-reduced-motion (text shown instantly).

customization.name: --chars
customization.description: Number of characters (set to your text length)

variations: Loop with multiple phrases via JS
Solid block caret instead of bar
Remove caret after typing with a forwards fill
effects.ai_usage: Great for hero taglines and terminal/CLI aesthetics. Pure-CSS version requires: a monospace font, the text wrapped in an inline-block element, ch-based width animation, and --chars set to the exact character count. For dynamic/looping text, prefer a tiny JS loop that swaps textContent. Keep white-space:nowrap and overflow:hidden.
effects.source: Hello world

effects.title: Accordion Expand
effects.summary: A native <details> accordion with a smoothly animated open/close using grid-rows.
effects.description: Builds the accordion on the semantic <details>/<summary> element and animates the panel height with the grid-template-rows 0fr→1fr technique (which animates from auto height cleanly), plus a rotating chevron.
effects.browser_support: Modern browsers; the 0fr→1fr grid animation works in current Chromium/Firefox/Safari. Older browsers simply jump (acceptable).
effects.performance_notes: Animates grid-template-rows; the content wrapper must be overflow:hidden. No JS needed for toggle (native details).
effects.accessibility_notes: Built on <details>/<summary>, so it's keyboard-operable and announced as a disclosure out of the box — no ARIA needed. Honors prefers-reduced-motion.
variations: Exclusive accordion (name attribute on <details> in supporting browsers)
effects.ai_usage: Use for FAQs and settings disclosures. Prefer the native <details>/<summary> for free accessibility. To animate the open height, wrap panel content in a div and use display:grid; grid-template-rows:0fr on closed and 1fr on [open], with the wrapper overflow:hidden. Rotate the chevron on [open]. For 'only one open', use the shared name attribute on <details> where supported. Reduced-motion: drop the transition.
effects.source:
What is Style Catalog? ▾

A museum and catalogue of web effects, built for AI agents.

How do I add an effect? ▾

Run npm run new, edit the file, then npm run build.

effects.title: Floating Bob
effects.summary: A gentle up-and-down hover float that makes hero illustrations feel alive.
effects.description: An infinite ease-in-out translateY loop (optionally with a soft shadow that breathes) gives an object a weightless, floating quality.
effects.performance_notes: Transform-only loop — GPU-friendly and cheap.
effects.accessibility_notes: Ambient motion; disable under prefers-reduced-motion. Avoid on text the user must read precisely.
variations: Add a synced shadow that grows as it rises
Slight rotation for a drifting feel
Different delays for a group of floats
effects.ai_usage: Use on hero illustrations, mascots, icons, and badges to add life. Apply .float; the keyframe eases translateY between 0 and -var(--amp). Give grouped elements different --dur and animation-delay so they don't move in lockstep. Mandatory reduced-motion guard.

effects.title: Modal Fade & Scale
effects.summary: A dialog that fades its backdrop and scales up from 95%, using the native <dialog> element.
effects.description: Uses the semantic <dialog> (with showModal()) for built-in focus trapping and backdrop, animating the panel from a slightly-scaled, transparent state and fading ::backdrop.
effects.performance_notes: Transform/opacity transition; trivial.
effects.accessibility_notes: The native <dialog> with showModal() gives focus trapping, Escape-to-close, and inert background for free — strongly preferred over a div-based modal. Return focus to the trigger on close (handled here).
effects.ai_usage: Use the NATIVE <dialog> for modals — call dialog.showModal() (not just .show()) to get focus trapping, ::backdrop, and Escape handling for free. Animate the panel (opacity + transform: scale) and ::backdrop opacity via an [open] class or the dialog[open] state; manage display with @starting-style or by toggling a class after showModal. Always return focus to the trigger on close. The demo auto-reopens to show the transition.
effects.source:
Native dialog

Focus-trapped, Escape-closable, animated.

effects.title: Morphing Blob
effects.summary: An organic shape slowly morphs by animating border-radius — no SVG needed.
effects.description: Animating the eight-value border-radius syntax between several lopsided states makes a single div ooze between organic blob shapes, popular as decorative hero accents.
effects.performance_notes: Animating border-radius repaints but on a single element it's fine. Add a slow rotation for extra life.
effects.accessibility_notes: Decorative; disable under prefers-reduced-motion.
variations: Use as an image mask (clip an to the blob)
Add a slow rotate for more motion
effects.ai_usage: Decorative hero accents, avatar frames, or image masks. Apply .blob; the keyframes interpolate the long-form border-radius (h-radii / v-radii) between asymmetric values. Combine a slow rotate to avoid the morph looking like simple pulsing. To mask an image, give the the same border-radius animation or use it as a clip shape.

effects.title: Scroll Reveal Fade-Up
effects.summary: Elements fade and slide up as they enter the viewport, via IntersectionObserver.
effects.description: Items start hidden (opacity 0, translateY); an IntersectionObserver adds an 'in' class when they scroll into view, triggering a CSS transition. The web-standard, dependency-free replacement for scroll libraries.
effects.performance_notes: Far cheaper than scroll-event listeners; the observer fires only at thresholds. Transition animates opacity/transform.
effects.accessibility_notes: Content is in the DOM and accessible regardless of reveal state. Under prefers-reduced-motion, show everything immediately (no transform).

customization.name: threshold
customization.description: How much must be visible to trigger

effects.ai_usage: The standard scroll-entrance for marketing pages. Give items class .reveal (opacity:0; translateY). Create one IntersectionObserver, observe all .reveal nodes, and add .in on intersection (optionally unobserve to fire once). Stagger with transition-delay tied to index. ALWAYS short-circuit to the visible state under prefers-reduced-motion. The demo auto-scrolls so the effect is visible in the catalog frame.
effects.source:
Reveal 1
Reveal 2
Reveal 3
Reveal 4
Reveal 5

effects.title: Shake (Attention / Error)
effects.summary: A quick horizontal shake to flag an error or invalid input.
effects.description: A short keyframe wobbles translateX left/right a few times then settles — the universal 'wrong password' / validation-error cue. Demo re-triggers on a timer (and on click).
effects.performance_notes: Single short transform animation; negligible.
effects.accessibility_notes: Motion is brief but should be paired with a text error message and aria-live, not used alone. Reduce-motion users get a static color flash instead of shaking.
variations: Combine with a red border flash
effects.ai_usage: Trigger on form validation failure by toggling a .shake class (remove then re-add on the next frame to restart the animation, or listen for animationend to clean up). Always accompany with a visible, aria-live error message — motion alone is not accessible. Under reduced motion, flash the border/background color instead of translating.
effects.source: Invalid — click or wait to shake

effects.title: Fluid Typography (clamp)
effects.summary: Type that scales smoothly with the viewport using clamp() — no breakpoints.
effects.description: Demonstrates clamp(min, preferred-vw, max) for headings/body so font sizes interpolate fluidly between a minimum and maximum across screen widths, eliminating media-query font jumps.
effects.performance_notes: Pure CSS; recalculated on resize by the engine — free.
effects.accessibility_notes: Include a rem term in the preferred value (e.g. 1rem + 2vw) so text still responds to user zoom/font-size; a pure-vw value can break zoom. Respect the min so text never gets too small.
variations: Fluid spacing/margins with the same technique
Combine with a modular scale for fluid steps
effects.ai_usage: Use to make headings responsive WITHOUT media queries. Pattern: font-size: clamp(MIN, REM + VW, MAX). ALWAYS include a rem term in the middle (e.g. 1rem + 4vw) so the text still scales with user zoom — a pure vw value is an accessibility failure. Pick MIN/MAX from your type scale. Resize the catalog frame / window to see it interpolate.
effects.source:
Resize me, I flow

Fluid type with clamp(2rem, 1rem + 5vw, 4.5rem) — smooth scaling, no breakpoints, still zoomable.

effects.title: Font Pairings
effects.summary: Reference combinations of heading + body fonts using only system font stacks.
effects.description: Shows several tasteful heading/body pairings (serif display + sans body, geometric + humanist, monospace accent) built from cross-platform system font stacks so they need no web-font downloads.
effects.browser_support: All browsers (system font stacks resolve per OS).
effects.performance_notes: System fonts = zero network cost and no layout shift. The fastest possible typography.
effects.accessibility_notes: System stacks respect user/OS font settings. Ensure adequate size and line-height for readability (1.5 for body).
variations: Swap a system stack for a self-hosted web font
Pair weights, not just families, within one superfamily
effects.ai_usage: A reference for pairing fonts WITHOUT web-font downloads. Define --font-head and --font-body as system stacks and apply to headings/body. Principles shown: contrast families (serif heading + sans body), or stay in one family with weight contrast. Prefer system stacks for performance; only add a web font when brand truly requires it (then preload + font-display: swap). Copy a card's two stacks as your tokens.
effects.source:
Editorial Serif

Serif display over a clean sans body — classic, trustworthy, great for long-form reading.

head: Georgia, serif · body: system-ui, sans-serif

Modern Geometric

A bold geometric sans heading paired with a serif body for an unexpected, refined contrast.

head: Trebuchet MS, sans-serif · body: Georgia, serif

Technical Mono

Monospace heading signals a developer/technical product; sans body keeps it readable.

head: ui-monospace, monospace · body: system-ui, sans-serif

effects.title: Modular Type Scale
effects.summary: A harmonious heading/body size scale generated from one ratio, as copy-ready tokens.
effects.description: A reference specimen showing a modular scale (each step = previous × ratio, e.g. 1.25 'major third') mapped to --text-xs … --text-4xl tokens, plus a rendered preview of each step.
effects.performance_notes: Variables + static text; free.
effects.accessibility_notes: Use rem so user font-size settings scale everything. Keep body text >= 1rem (16px). Maintain a clear size jump between heading levels for hierarchy.

customization.name: --ratio
customization.description: Scale ratio (1.2 minor third, 1.25 major third, 1.333 perfect fourth, 1.618 golden)

variations: Use calc() chains or Sass to derive steps
effects.ai_usage: A copy-paste typographic scale. Choose a --ratio (1.2-1.333 for UI, up to 1.618 for editorial) and derive each token from the base: --text-lg = base*ratio, --text-xl = base*ratio^2, etc. Express in rem so OS/user zoom works. Reference these tokens for all font-size declarations instead of arbitrary px. Keep body >= 16px and ensure adjacent heading levels are visually distinct.
effects.source:
--text-4xlDisplay
--text-3xlHeading 1
--text-2xlHeading 2
--text-xlHeading 3
--text-lgLead paragraph
--text-baseBody text · ratio 1.25 (major third)
--text-smCaption / small print

effects.title: Readable Measure & Rhythm
effects.summary: Reference for line length (measure), line-height, and line-clamp — the rules that make body text readable.
effects.description: A specimen demonstrating the readability fundamentals: a 60-75 character measure via max-width: Nch, a comfortable line-height, and multi-line truncation with -webkit-line-clamp.
effects.browser_support: All modern browsers; line-clamp uses -webkit-line-clamp (cross-browser despite the prefix).
effects.performance_notes: Static; free.
effects.accessibility_notes: A 60-75ch measure and ~1.5 line-height materially improve readability for everyone, especially users with dyslexia/low vision. Don't clamp essential content without a way to read the rest.
effects.ai_usage: Apply these readability defaults to all body copy: set the text container max-width to ~60-75ch (the 'measure'); use line-height ~1.5-1.65 for body; for card excerpts, clamp with display:-webkit-box; -webkit-line-clamp:N; -webkit-box-orient:vertical; overflow:hidden. Never let body text run the full width of a wide viewport. Don't clamp content that has no 'read more' path.
effects.source:
max-width: 60ch · line-height: 1.6

A comfortable measure

Lines of 60 to 75 characters are easiest to read: the eye tracks from the end of one line to the start of the next without losing its place. Constrain body text with a ch-based max-width instead of letting it span the whole viewport.

-webkit-line-clamp: 2

This excerpt is clamped to two lines and ends with an ellipsis, which is perfect for card summaries and list previews where space is limited and the full text lives elsewhere.
